title: Game News Round-Up
summary: Daily curated games-industry news digest for 7 October 2024.

items.title: **Games Done Quick** to hold Hurricane Helene disaster fundraiser next week
bullets: Donations will go towards humanitarian aid organisation, Direct Relief.

items.title: **Halo’s 343** rebrands and confirms ‘multiple games’ coming, using Unreal Engine 5
bullets: Currently working on "multiple projects" in Unreal Engine 5
The studio claims it's changed its culture and workflow

items.title: **Halo Infinite** is getting a third-person mode
bullets: The new viewpoint is coming to the game next month

Third-person controls will be “supported at a mode level”, senior community manager John ‘Unyshek’ Junyszek said. This means players won’t be able to manually switch between first-person and third-person mode – it will be set for them.
The third-person view will also be added to Forge mode allowing creators to switch individual players (or all players) between first- and third-person perspectives as desired.

items.title: The **Until Dawn** movie has finished filming
bullets: The film is planned for release in October 2025

items.title: **Astro Bot**’s 87-song soundtrack is now available on streaming platforms
bullets: The soundtrack includes PlayStation homages such as "Bot of War"
The majority of tracks were composed by Kenny CM Young. Additional tracks are contributed by Hirofumi Sasaki, Tadashi Yatabe, Kemmei Adachi, and Sachiko Miyano.

items.title: **Epic** bringing free games to its **mobile store** in a bid to entice new users
bullets: Arriving alongside third-party apps in Q4

In total, Epic expects to have "between 10 and 50" third-party applications available on its mobile store by this "holiday season", with Ark: Ultimate Mobile Edition already to confirmed as one of those games

When it comes to enticing developers, it's also pushing a similar self-publishing strategy to that seen on PC, alongside a reduced cut of earnings on app sales and in-app purchases - 12 percent - compared to Apple and Google's share. Epic also waives its cut entirely for six months if a developer agrees to release its game as a six-month Epic store exclusive.
